diagrams.title: Requirements Model Components Overview
diagrams.description: Shows the five primary components of an analysis/requirements model and their relationships
elements: Scenario-Based Modeling (Use Cases) - captures user interactions
Class-Based Modeling (Object Analysis) - captures static structure
diagrams.purpose: Provides visual overview of how different modeling techniques contribute to complete requirements specification and their interconnections

diagrams.title: Class Relationship Types Comparison
diagrams.description: Visual comparison of Association, Aggregation, Composition, and Generalization with UML notation
elements: Association: simple line connecting classes, multiplicity at ends
Aggregation: line with hollow diamond at 'whole' end (e.g., Department◇--Teachers)
Composition: line with filled diamond at 'whole' end (e.g., Book◆--Pages)
Generalization: solid line with hollow arrow toward parent (e.g., SavingsAccount──▲BankAccount)
diagrams.purpose: Clarifies the distinctions between relationship types with concrete examples, helping students choose appropriate relationships when modeling

diagrams.title: Use Case Structure Diagram
diagrams.description: Internal structure of a use case showing Basic Flow, Alternative Flows, and relationships
elements: Basic Flow: main success scenario arrow
Alternative Flows: branching arrows from basic flow
Preconditions (before use case), Postconditions (after use case)
Includes: extensions, special requirements, business rules
diagrams.purpose: Shows how to structure use case descriptions beyond simple oval diagrams, emphasizing the detailed flow documentation needed for requirements

diagrams.title: Requirements Traceability Matrix Template
diagrams.description: Sample traceability matrix showing requirements linked to design, code, and test artifacts
elements: Columns: Design Documents, Code Modules, Test Cases
Cells: Traceability links (X or artifact IDs)
Coverage indicators: marked vs unmarked cells
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates practical traceability matrix structure and helps students understand how to maintain and evaluate traceability coverage
